blurb: A tough, glaucous-leaved prairie native that holds its smooth blue-green foliage clean all season, then erupts in clouds of lavender-blue, yellow-eyed daisies in fall — one of the last great pollinator plants of the year.
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
spread: Clumping; short rhizomes spread it slowly, also reseeds
seasons: Smooth blue-green clasping foliage spring–summer; lavender-blue daisies Aug–Oct; tan seed plumes into winter
wildlife: Major late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies; seeds for birds
toxic: No documented toxicity; grown as an ornamental native, not a food plant

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — rated USDA zones 3–8, so Boulder's zone 5b–6a winters are no concern for this wide-ranging prairie native. The stems die to the ground after hard frost and re-sprout from the root crown and short rhizomes in spring.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container stock in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into lean, well-drained ground; it establishes quickly and is easy. In containers use a gritty, free-draining mix, site in full sun, and don't let it sit wet — it sulks in soggy soil and over-rich mixes make it flop. Once sited it persists for years and reseeds.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun for the sturdiest, most upright stems and heaviest fall bloom. In too much shade or rich soil the stems stretch and lean and may need staking.[1]
care.soil: Average, dry to medium, well-drained soil; it tolerates shallow rocky and calcareous ground and is at home on Boulder's lean alkaline clay so long as drainage is decent. Avoid wet, heavy clay. Don't over-amend — rich soil produces floppy growth.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established. Water through the first season to settle the roots, then little supplemental water is needed in our semi-arid climate except in prolonged drought.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 18–24 in. apart; mature clumps reach 1–2 ft wide and 2–4 ft tall. Short rhizomes let it widen slowly into a colony over time.[1]
care.propagation: Easy from seed and by division. Cold-moist stratify the seed (or fall-sow outdoors for natural winter chilling) and surface-sow — aster seed needs light and barely any cover. Divide established clumps in early spring every few years to rejuvenate and to make more plants; it spreads by short rhizomes, so divisions take readily.[1][2]
care.sow: Surface-sow onto firmed soil and press in — the fine seed needs light to germinate. Fall-sow outdoors for natural stratification, or chill moist seed before a late-winter start. Seedlings reach blooming size their second year.[1]
care.stratify: A cold-moist period improves and evens out germination; about 30–60 days at refrigerator temperature works, and fall sowing supplies it naturally outdoors.[1]
care.bloom: Blooms late summer into fall — roughly August through October on the Front Range — in sprays of lavender-blue, yellow-centered daisies that age to reddish-purple discs. It is one of the latest forbs to flower here, a crucial bridge for pollinators before frost.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Low. Pinch or cut the stems back by about half in early June to make a bushier, self-supporting plant and reduce the need to stake. Leave the seed heads standing for birds and winter structure, then cut to the ground in late winter before new growth.[1]
care.selfsow: Reseeds modestly where soil is open, and creeps slowly outward by short rhizomes; neither is aggressive. Deadhead before seed set if you want to limit volunteers, or leave heads up to feed birds and self-sow.[1][2]
care.troubles: No serious insect or disease problems. Powdery mildew and rust can appear in crowded, humid, or shaded plantings — full sun and good airflow prevent it. Tall plants in rich soil or shade may flop; the early-June cutback fixes that.[1]
care.companions: A classic prairie partner for goldenrods (Solidago) and perennial sunflowers (Helianthus), whose gold contrasts with its blue, plus little bluestem and other warm-season grasses. Excellent in native, pollinator, and cottage borders for late-season color.[1]

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental native pollinator plant; no documented use as a food.[1]
edible.caution: Smooth aster is not documented as a food plant and is not eaten. It is not notably poisonous to people or pets, but there is no established edible use — treat it as ornamental and don't eat it.[1]
